metadata.title: Color‐blind and racially suppressive discourses on <scp>German‐speaking</scp> Twitter: A mixed method analysis of the Hanau White nationalist shootings
metadata.journal: Journal of Community & Applied Social Psychology

abstract:
This study examined German-speaking Twitter users' reckoning with racism in response to the right-wing extremist shootings that targeted ethnic minorities in Hanau, Germany. We employed a mixed-methods analysis combining Structural Topic Modelling (STM) and discourse analysis.

We used STM to identify broad topic patterns and focused on the topics involving the racialized commentary the German-speaking public offered in the aftermath of Hanau.

The STM analysis resulted in 50 topics; among these conversations, racism in German society and racism: threats and fears were among the top 10 topics. In total, 36.8% of the 50 topics in the first month of the shootings were racismrelated discussions but the majority of these discussions did not describe the Hanau shootings as racially motivated. We conducted a discourse analysis to capture a more finegrained understanding of the users' racialized conversations, focusing specifically on the discursive contexts in which racially suppressive discourses were used and taking place.

We identified three racially suppressive discourses, including the explanation that the shootings/shooter was mentally ill, might be racially motivated, and a product of extremist groups. This study uncovered multifaceted public conversations in the aftermath of Hanau and offered interpretations of large-scale conversations that reckoned with racism on German-speaking Twitter.

paragraphs: sustains that these logics/frames are "the result of talking about race in a world that insists that race does not matter, rather than being a tool of color-blindness. However, since it is so preeminent in whites' race talk, it must be included as part of the linguistic modalities of color-blind racism" (p. 71).

Subsequent research has explored how White people carry out the habitually systemic work of colour-blindness (Bell & Hartmann, 2007;Mueller, 2017). Research has chronicled how "well-intentioned" White liberals (Trepagnier, 2010) and pro-diversity views (Burke, 2012) often unintentionally reinscribe the primacy of Whiteness.

Likewise, in an analysis of undergraduate research papers on racial inequality and intergenerational wealth, Mueller (2017) notes that White students developed colour-blind logics or "white epistemic maneuvers" of ignorance to divert racial awareness. Collectively, this work suggests that the structure of White supremacy requires, according to critical race scholar Charles Mills (2007), "an epistemology of ignorance."

Discourse analytic scholars have also documented how members of mainstream groups utilize and draw on colour-blindness discourses to make non-racial claims. Augoustinos and Every (2007) have noted specific discursive strategies associated with this type of race talk, including discursive deracialization or the absence of the topic of race from a conversation that could be viewed through the lens of race. Bertrand's (2010) and Durheim and Dixon's work (2010) illustrate the specific deracialization strategy of using place names and metaphors like "affluent" and "diverse population" that index explicit discussions about race. Researchers have also noted that people often draw on talk of "fit" and "the inability to integrate" to de-racialize racist discourse (Capdevila & Callaghan, 2008;Goodman & Burke, 2011). Van Dijk's (1993a, 1993b) classic texts have also noted that accusations of racism are often discursively tricky, reserved only for the most extreme cases or perpetrated by people in other places, leading to the use of different terms. This feature of talk has been noted in Goodman and Burke's (2010) investigation of talk about refugee asylum seekers in the U.K., where opposition to asylum was "possibly approaching xenophobia," but not "racist" (p. 4).

sections.paper_id: Windel et al. - 2023 - Color‐blind and racially suppressive discourses on German‐spe
sections.header_raw: | COLOUR-BLINDNESS AND RACISM AS EXCEPTION IN GERMANY
sections.header_norm: colour blindness and racism as exception in germany
paragraphs: Like colour-blindness, racism in Germany tends to be understood as existing in exceptional cases (Terkessidis, 2010), in the "bad" others within German society like members of Neo-Nazi groups, followers of the party "Alternative for Germany" (AfD), and the "National Democratic Party of German" (NPD) (Ogette, 2019). Anti-racism scholar, Tupoka Ogette (2019) explains this conceptualization by speaking to the population of White Germans who mean well and believe that racism exists in the intentional, evil practices of a few. She calls this population "residents of Happyland" (Ogette, 2019, p. 23) and describes how well-meaning White Germans do little to acknowledge that racism is part of their everyday world. When accused of racism, Happylanders defensively respond and claim their niceness. Thus, for many White Germans, racism exists in the evil exceptional others, but not themselves.

Fatima El-Tayeb adds to this discussion in her analysis of how the process of actively forgetting German and European histories of racist violence constructs racist events as singular without contexts, origins, and impacts (2011). Thus, each event is seen as an exception and met with surprise. Colour-blindness is key to this process in which Europe "prohibits discourses around racialized oppression" (El-Tayeb, 2011, p. xvii)

paragraphs: It is across this complex context that the Hanau shootings occurred. On the evening of February 19, 2020, 43-yearold Tobias Rathjen shot people in and around two shisha bars in Hanau, a small town near Frankfurt, Germany, killing nine people and injuring several others. The people murdered belonged to various immigrant communities in Germany, including Kurdish, Sinti, Turkish, Afghan, Bulgarian, Bosnian, and Romanian communities. After the two shisha bar shootings, Rathjen went to his apartment and killed his mother and himself. His father escaped the shootings. Analyses of Rathjen's manifesto and confession letter showed a far-right racist motivation for the killing. Caniglia, Winkler, and Métais (2020) analysed the manifesto and found White supremacist theories related to the superiority of the West's achievements that align with ideas expressed by the White supremacist shooters in Halle, Christchurch, and El Paso. The attacks were also widely circulated and reached international White supremacist and far-right audiences through Telegram chats and forums (Caniglia et al., 2020). The Hanau shootings are being treated as an act of right-wing terrorism and racially motivated. Investigations of the police's response on the night of the shootings, a locked emergency exit, and questions about why Rathjen could legally possess weapons despite his known mental health problems have largely been discontinued or rejected as of October 2022.

Hanau was part of a series of White nationalist right-wing murders and attacks in Germany, including the Halle Synagogue shooting in 2019, the murder of politician Walter Luebcke in 2019, and the investigations of the Nationalist Socialist Underground murders (2000)(2001)(2002)(2003)(2004)(2005)(2006)(2007). Right-wing extremist views have also re-entered popular political and cultural imagination through culturally acceptable ways, such as clothing laced with anti-Semitic and anti-Muslim symbols (Miller-Idriss, 2018). Germany has also seen an increase in public support for right-wing populist parties, like the AfD. However, unlike other White nationalist attacks, this violent episode brought about a rupture in the routine suppression of racial discourse where German politicians responded to the Hanau shootings by publicly declaring the event was motivated by racism. German Chancellor Angela Merkel led this national recognition and stated, "racism is poison" (2020).

sections.paper_id: Windel et al. - 2023 - Color‐blind and racially suppressive discourses on German‐spe
paragraphs: The above discussion of colour-blindness and public political acknowledgment of the Hanau shootings as a racist event demonstrate that an analysis of Twitter responses to Hanau can be an important contribution to how German-speaking Twitter users reckon with racism, specifically around colour-blindness. This paper has two research questions:
RQ1. How are everyday German-speaking Twitter users reckoning with racism in response to Hanau? RQ2. How and in what discursive contexts were racially suppressive discourses used and taking place?

We responded to these questions by first exploring the broad topic patterns and then asked what kind of racialized commentary, including colour-blindness, German-speaking Twitter users offer following this tragic event. To approach this volume of data, we employed a mixed-methods analysis combining STM and discourse analysis. STM is an unsupervised machine learning method that allows for the investigation of latent topics in voluminous, unstructured text data sets (Roberts et al., 2014). We used STM to identify broad topic patterns and focused on the topics involving the racialized commentary the German-speaking public offered in the aftermath of Hanau. We also conducted a discourse analysis to capture a more fine-grained understanding of the users' racialized conversations, focusing specifically on the extent to which racially suppressive discourses, were taking place. Integrating quantitative with qualitative analysis (e.g., discourse analysis) is complementary in many ways and enables an examination of large corpuses of naturalistic data, like Twitter (Aranda, Sele, Etchanchu, Guyt, & Vaara, 2021;Pérez Curiel & Lim on-Naharro, 2019;Pérez Curiel & Molpeceres, 2020;Törnberg & Törnberg, 2016;Van Dijk, 2015).

sections.paper_id: Windel et al. - 2023 - Color‐blind and racially suppressive discourses on German‐spe
paragraphs: Data for this study were extracted from Twitter, Inc using the REST and Premium archive Application Protocol Interfaces (APIs). An API is akin to a telephone line between software programs: in this instance, we used the Rtweet package (Kearney, 2020) in the R statistical software (R Core Team, 2014) to collect public German language tweets using the hashtags pertaining to the Hanau shootings. The REST API was used to collect real-time tweets for our initial hashtag identification, while the PREMIUM API (v.1) was used to collect all tweets used in the analysis during the 30-day period following the shootings (February 19, 2020-March 20, 2020), resulting in N = 710,890 unique tweets. We excluded tweets in languages other than German, spam, and irrelevant tweets, but retained tweets that included a mix of German and another language, as well as hashtags related to the shootings in English. We also omitted hyperlinks and excluded usernames to protect user anonymity. The final dataset had 61,199 unique tweets sent by 24,645 unique users.

paragraphs: We used STM to initially find broad topic patterns and then focused in on the racialized commentary offered in the aftermath of Hanau. After data cleaning, we employed STM to detect broad patterns and latent themes in the text data. Topic models are incorrect but useful text analysis algorithms that can aid in revealing broad patterns in a less resource intensive way than a human coder, which allows for quicker, more reliable analysis. In a topic model, a topic (K) can be understood as a set of words that share a common (often) interpretable theme. Its proportion number stands for the likelihood of the set of words being generated by that topic, and for the expected proportion of each topic across one document (one tweet). A topic model comprises a mixture of these different K's, summing to 100%.

Additionally, other document-specific factors like time variables can be included, which can affect both documenttopic proportion and word-distribution over topics. In our topic models, we incorporated the number of days from the shootings as a covariate to analyse how topic proportions vary over time.

Using the STM package to find the initial number of topics (K) resulted in a model with 63 topics. We ran the KSearch function in increments of five with K's ranging from 25 to 80 to find the number of K that reflected the most semantically coherent and distinctive number of topics (see Figure A1 in Appendix A for the full diagnostic analysis). Based on this criterion, we selected 50 as the number of K topics and then analysed the STM results with the R package stm_insights to validate, interpret, and visualize topic results (Schwemmer, 2018). We then read through the STM output of 50 representative tweets from each of the 50 topics (approximate n = 2500) (Nelson, 2020). Two of the author's then inferred content characteristics and independently generated a topic label.

For data visualization, we created a table that includes all topics with their labels, top words, and proportion (Table 1). Finally, we created a sub-table that focused in on the racialized commentary (Table 2).

STM was used as an entry point for the overarching analysis and as a sampling method for the following discourse analysis (Merrill & Åkerlund, 2018). We used a sample of tweets from the topics in the sub-table of racialized commentary to analyse in the discourse analysis. Specifically, the primary focus of our discourse analysis was to examine the discursive contexts in which racially suppressive discourses were used and taking place. Our analysis was informed by Van Leeuwen's (2009) framework for analysing representations of "social actors." Based on Van Leeuwen's assertion that discourses are recontextualizations of social practices, we examined the elements of users' explanations and accounts of Hanau as they related to race and racism in terms of: who is included and excluded, when, in what context, and how, linguistically. This involved careful reading of the tweets, with emphasis on putting them within a particular socio-political context. We first theorized the action orientation and functional dimensions regarding the varying ways that these racialized conversations were presented and accounted for in the aftermath of

We then moved beyond a close analysis of the tweets to the wider ideological context of participants' language use, namely to questions of what or whose interests are served by the specific constructions of racialized talk in the aftermath of Hanau. 6 | RESULTS 6.1 | STM of racialized commentary 6.1.1 | Topic proportions and thematic groupings Table 1 illustrates the STM model of 50 topics, with the topics ordered based on the highest topic proportion. Topics that included the word stem "rass" (for racism) are bolded, topics that included the word "recht" (for right-wing) and

AfD are italicized, and topics with the word nazi are underlined (T29 had both AfD and nazi included). As Table 1 shows, there were several regnant conversations that the German public expressed in the aftermath of the Hanau shootings. Among these conversations, racism in German society (T24) and racism: threats and fears (T32) were among the top 10, totalling 5.7% of topic conversations. T24 included conversations about the pervasiveness of racism in German societal spaces, and T32 involved discussions regarding the exclusion of Black people, people of colour, and migrants in Germany and how this exclusion perpetuates and produces racism, fear, and threats.

Table 2 provides more details of the racialized commentary from Table 1. Based on a deep reading of each racialized topic, we found six interconnected thematic groupings: (1) AfD's Responsibility (11.1%), (2) Everyday Racism (9%), (3) Lone Wolf/Isolated Case (5.2), (4) Mental Illness (4.8), (5) Critiquing News Coverage (4.3%), and (6) Organizing Vigils (2.4%). Thematic grouping entailed discussions involving: (1) AfD's responsibility because it normalized racist ideas in politics;
(2) the pervasiveness and everydayness of racism in German society;

(3) the shooter/shootings being a lone wolf incident disconnected from groups with White supremacist ideology; (4) the shooter being mentally ill and that racism was not a reason for the shootings; (5) a critique of the media's coverage of the shootings as not racist; and (6) people to join vigils in response to the racist event. In sum, 36.8% of the 50 topics in the first month of the Hanau shootings included racism-related conversations. At the same time, however, the majority of these racialized conversations did not directly name racism as pervasive in Germany or describe the Hanau shootings as racially motivated.

We then moved beyond a close analysis of the tweets to the wider ideological context of participants' language use, namely to questions of what or whose interests are served by the specific constructions of racialized talk in the aftermath of Hanau. 6 | RESULTS 6.1 | STM of racialized commentary 6.1.1 | Topic proportions and thematic groupings Table 1 illustrates the STM model of 50 topics, with the topics ordered based on the highest topic proportion. Topics that included the word stem "rass" (for racism) are bolded, topics that included the word "recht" (for right-wing) and

AfD are italicized, and topics with the word nazi are underlined (T29 had both AfD and nazi included). As Table 1 shows, there were several regnant conversations that the German public expressed in the aftermath of the Hanau shootings. Among these conversations, racism in German society (T24) and racism: threats and fears (T32) were among the top 10, totalling 5.7% of topic conversations. T24 included conversations about the pervasiveness of racism in German societal spaces, and T32 involved discussions regarding the exclusion of Black people, people of colour, and migrants in Germany and how this exclusion perpetuates and produces racism, fear, and threats.

Table 2 provides more details of the racialized commentary from Table 1. Based on a deep reading of each racialized topic, we found six interconnected thematic groupings: (1) AfD's Responsibility (11.1%), (2) Everyday Racism (9%), (3) Lone Wolf/Isolated Case (5.2), (4) Mental Illness (4.8), (5) Critiquing News Coverage (4.3%), and (6) Organizing Vigils (2.4%). Thematic grouping entailed discussions involving: (1) AfD's responsibility because it normalized racist ideas in politics;

(2) the pervasiveness and everydayness of racism in German society;

(3) the shooter/shootings being a lone wolf incident disconnected from groups with White supremacist ideology; (4) the shooter being mentally ill and that racism was not a reason for the shootings; (5) a critique of the media's coverage of the shootings as not racist; and (6) people to join vigils in response to the racist event. In sum, 36.8% of the 50 topics in the first month of the Hanau shootings included racism-related conversations. At the same time, however, the majority of these racialized conversations did not directly name racism as pervasive in Germany or describe the Hanau shootings as racially motivated.
sections.preview_head: We used STM to initially find broad topic patterns and then focused in on the racialized commentary offered in the aftermath of Hanau. After data cleaning, we employed STM to detect broad patterns and latent themes in the text data. Topic models are incorrect but useful text analysis algorithms that can aid in revealing broad patterns in a less resource intensive way than a human coder, which allows for quicker, more reliable analysis.

sections.paper_id: Windel et al. - 2023 - Color‐blind and racially suppressive discourses on German‐spe
paragraphs: To understand important moments in time-related to Hanau, Figure 1 shows the topic changes, starting on the day of the shootings and ending 4 weeks after. Notably, most of the Twitter content was posted during the first 3 days.

A second spike occurred on March 4 and 5, likely due to the official vigil that took place on March 4, 2020, where politicians, family, and community members gave speeches to remember the victims of the shootings.

We also conducted a time analysis of the two highest racialized topic proportion topics from Table 2 that included the word racism/racist in the topic label (T24) and in the top words (T38) (see Figures 2 and 3). Topics 24 was the highest in the first 2 days, followed by days 11 and 12, and 29.
In contrast, Topic 38 has a different flow where day one is quite high but decreases drastically right after to then slowly rise until day 30.

These two topics are related but refer to different discussions of racism. Topic 24 is part of the thematic group "everyday racism." The Hanau shootings were an event that received a lot of attention in the first days. In the "everyday racism" topic, people named racism as part of German society, but it seems like this might be a "trend,"

particularly, because it was discussed at the beginning of the event but was not followed up after the first few days of the event. This raises the question of performative allyship, where people, specifically people in power, exclaim solidarity without practicing "direct and measurable changes" (Sylvain, Knochel, Gingles, & Catagnus, 2022, p. 1). We ran other topics (e.g., T32) from the "everyday racism" thematic grouping and they showed a similar pattern. In contrast, topic 38 demonstrated continuous organizing to remember the victims of the event and to continue naming the racism of this event.

sections.paper_id: Windel et al. - 2023 - Color‐blind and racially suppressive discourses on German‐spe
sections.header_raw: | Racially suppressive discourses in the aftermath of a White Nationalist shooting
sections.header_norm: racially suppressive discourses in the aftermath of a white nationalist shooting
paragraphs: As the previous section demonstrated, the circumstances surrounding the Hanau shootings appear to be highly conducive to an accusation of racist intent. And yet, our Twitter dataset included instances of denials in describing this incident as racist. Hence, the primary focus of our discourse analysis was to examine the discursive contexts in which racially suppressive discourses were used and taking place. We focus on tweets where alternative explanations to racism were given in response to the Hanau shootings. We identified three racially suppressive discourses used to make non-racist/downplaying racist explanations and accusations, including the explanation that the Hanau White Nationalist shootings/shooter was: (1) mentally ill, (2) might be racially motivated, and (3) a product of extremist groups. The tweets below best illustrate these discourses. Additional example tweets are provided in the Appendix A. Tweet 2: Tobias R. was clearly ruled by delusions. R. is not a right-wing extremist, not terrorist, R. is a prime example of a paranoid person.

In these extracts, there is an explicit charge and privileging of mental illness as a motive over right-wing extremism, which aligns with normative constructions of race-based violence in Germany. This is brought about by contrasting the non-racist and racist explanation: "the perpetrator was mentally ill, not a right-wing extremist;" "Tobias R was clearly ruled by delusions. R is not a right-wing extremist." Edwards (1997) and Dickerson (2000) argue that drawing contrasts accomplishes specific discursive goals, including contesting category assignment, positioning the self/group in a positive light, and justifying claims. The non-racist explanation is also accomplished through the use of lexicon descriptors of certainty. For example, the use of "clearly" and the naming of "schizophrenia" by the first tweeter gives the explanation of mental illness more weight. It presents mental illness as credible and as an accurate reflection and explanation of the Hanau shootings. These categorical distinctions and modalizing features of determinism produce discursive deracialization and function to present right-wing extremism and racism as unlikely explanations. Similar to discourses of ignorance found in race talk (Henriques, 1984), assigning blame to mental illness may allow for the circumventing of racialized accusation and correction (Duxbury, Frizzell, & Lindsay, 2018;Metzl & MacLeish, 2015) and downplays historical ideologies and structures of White supremacy in German society (Kauhanen, 2020). The tweets also speak to the racialized terrain within lone wolf discourses, where White people, in particular, are often exempt from responsibility for their violent crimes, while Black people, people of colour, and
Muslim shooters tend to be identified as terrorists (Beydoun, 2018;Penny, 2011). It is what Corbin (2017) calls "the presumption of White innocence" (p. 469). The following is another example:

Tweet 3: The killer is obviously mentally ill. In his letter of confession he speaks of aliens, he accused Hollywood of stealing his ideas for films, he can remember the day he was born and he saw himself being pursued by secret services. Terrible.

As with the previous tweets, a noticeable feature is the use of lexicon descriptors of certainty. The killer is obviously mentally ill. Where this tweet is different listing of characteristics upon which a mental illness explanation is based. In particular, the user lists the behaviours of the shooter that are obviously "abnormal" (e.g., speaks with aliens) in contrast to obviously "normal" behaviour. Interestingly, scholars analysing discourses of majority groups have noted similar discursive features to how certain behaviours and characteristics of minority groups are constructed as abnormal making them acceptable targets of racism (Verkuyten, 2001). In this context, abnormalization is doing something different. Through the strategy of abnornmalization, a racist explanation gets backgrounded where the user is producing a colour-blind version of the world, allowing the user to talk about the Hanau shootings without ever acknowledging racial difference in Germany. Consequently, colour-blindness has the effect of reinforcing and legitimizing the racial status quo. Other users took on a slightly different evaluative tone: In this tweet, there is a direct naming of Hanau as a matter of racism: All racist Nazi terrorist: mentally ill. Even though racism is named, the user does not go as far as to say something as direct as "this is racist" and instead lists mental illness of other extremist shootings that convey mental illness, not racism, as the real problem. Examples are routine and powerful discursive devices used in language. They can give grounds for specific ideas because they make them precise, hard to question, and credible (Edwards & Potter, 1992). Furthermore, the user reproduces the well-known neoliberalist trope that racism is rooted in the biased stuff of individuals (Salter & Adams, 2013).

Neoliberalist tropes reduce racism to the "characteristics of sick individuals" (Doane, 2014) and "act as tools for laundering, White-washing, and obscuring evidence of racism" as a systemic force (Salter & Adams, 2013, p. 785).

There were other tweets where users evoked mental illness and constructed the Hanau shootings as not racist. One of the main ways that this was done by recontextualizing certain behaviours, actions, and events and decontextualizing racialized behaviours and actions so that racism disappears:

Tweet 5: What is not asked: Why didn't he have help? Why wasn't he taken seriously? Why was he allowed to carry his weapons? Why couldn't the crime be prevented? How was his psyche? Why couldn't be communicate?

paragraphs: Other tweets were more dilemmatic and vacillated between diverging evaluations. In these instances, the shooter might have mental health issues and might have been racially motivated. The dataset included several tweets in which these multiple evaluations formed the basis of tweeters' constructions of the Hanau shootings:
Tweet 6: Everyone is asking themselves: Was the perpetrator of Hanau a right-wing radical or a confused psycho? Why not both?! And also put a whole lot of neoliberalism on top.

In this tweet, mental illness as a motive constitutes a more peripheral positioning than in previous tweets. The user does not exactly align with either position, but points out the possibility of both explanations by drawing on a particular pattern of language: posing a question. Questions play an important function in language beyond just the exchange of information. Questions often imply rhetorical answers or are answered by the language that follows (Black, 1992). Black (1992) notes that rhetorical questions, similar to the one being posed here, for which a questioner does not expect a firsthand answer, are asked for the persuasive effect of the asking and to lead listeners to a particular thought or appeal. The question of racial motivation versus an affirmation of racial motivation subtly helps to de-emphasize racism. Other scholars have noted that these types of formulations leave unanswered questions about the causes, consequences, and gravity of racism (Augoustinos, Tuffin, & Every, 2005).

A second way users deployed a "it might be racism" discourse was by evoking racism as a motive and then expressing counter-arguments. The following example shows how a Twitter user uses a disclaimer to explain the Hanau shootings:
Tweet 7: Mentally ill, mentally ill, mentally ill…Yes, he had racist views, but he was gripped by paranoid delusions. Still. It's a terrible act. RIP.

The user de-emphasizes the racialized context of the Hanau shootings by using the word but that downplays racism. Goffman (1967) theorized that these forms of talk are types of impression management that people use to make a good impression or to avoid a bad one. Van Dijk (1993a) described these semantic moves as "disclaimers," which are theorized to be a particular strategy that contributes to a positive self-image or defuses problematic statements.

Other tweets were more dilemmatic and vacillated between diverging evaluations. In these instances, the shooter might have mental health issues and might have been racially motivated. The dataset included several tweets in which these multiple evaluations formed the basis of tweeters' constructions of the Hanau shootings:

Tweet 6: Everyone is asking themselves: Was the perpetrator of Hanau a right-wing radical or a confused psycho? Why not both?! And also put a whole lot of neoliberalism on top.

The user does not exactly align with either position, but points out the possibility of both explanations by drawing on a particular pattern of language: posing a question. paragraphs: Finally, Twitter users made non-racist/downplayed racist explanations and accusations by locating racism in extreme settings typified by political regimes:
Tweet 8: The AfD is to blame. Fault. Fault. Fault. No more excuses. Your fault.

This tweet is noteworthy and when read through the lens of colour-blindness, places the source of contemporary racialized violence to the extremes that allows for a discussion of racialized violence in some and not to the everydayness of asymmetrical realities and histories of racial difference in Germany (Bonilla-Silva, 2006/2014). The following are more examples where users directly accuse the AfD and "Patriotic Europeans Against the Islamicization of the Occident" (Pegida) 1 for stirring up hate in German society and thus put direct responsibility on the party:

Tweet 9: Hate is in AfD's method, strategy. It is to instill the society with hate until the seed is sown. Alternative is not this party, unless hate is supposed to be an alternative. Thus, AfD clearly bears the guilt for the victims' deaths. In Hanau and others, AfD wants to hunt, destroy, and agitate.
Tweet 10: Hanau is a product of hate and baiting, AfD and Pegida are part of it. Arsonists are those who whistle populists, promote violence.

These tweets follow a similar structure and locate the Hanau shootings as a product of extremism. Both tweets are also highly accusatory, yet they at no point accuse the AfD or Pegida directly of racism. Similar to Van Dijk's (1993a, 1993b) work, other terms are used: hate, destroy, arsonists. The seeming disappearance of racism may be a product of the ideological context where interpersonal failings (hatred, baiting) can/are named, but structural dis/advantage based around "race" cannot/aren't.

sections.paper_id: Windel et al. - 2023 - Color‐blind and racially suppressive discourses on German‐spe
paragraphs: This study considered how German-speaking Twitter users' reckoned with racism and used racially suppressive discourses in response to the White nationalist shootings that occurred on February 19, 2020, in Hanau, Germany. The STM analysis resulted in 50 topics of which racism in German society and racism: threats and fears were among the top 10 topics. In total, 36.8% of the 50 topics in the first month of the Hanau shootings were racism-related discussions, though the majority of these discussions did not name racism as pervasive in German society or describe the Hanau shootings as racist. Together, our STM analysis makes an important contribution to how social media can be utilized to understand how society makes meaning and understands their world across time. Specifically, we argue that our analysis allows for a broader understanding of how Twitter users, who are a small part of the German-speaking population, make sense of nationalist, racist, and right-wing extremist violence.

Our discourse analysis captured a more fine-grained understanding of users' racialized conversations, focusing specifically on the extent to which racially suppressive discourses took place. German-speaking Twitter users constructed racism in the aftermath of Hanau by not naming racism directly, moving between diverging explanations of Hanau, and perpetuated by extremism or mental illness. Few studies have examined the routine association between mental illness and White nationalist violence. Among this small body of work, Penny (2011) provided an editorial examination of such associations within the context of the media's reaction to the killing of 80 people in Norway. Penny (2011) observes that the initial description of the incident was the work of "Islamist terrorists," but when the killer was identified as White Christian and European, the killings were then described as the work of "a madman."

Extending these ideas, interpreting Hanau as the work of a mental illness, we suggest functions to place the killer and Germany outside the realm of political and personal responsibility. Penny's (2011) analysis also highlights the racialized terrain entrenched in the typical association of mental illness and mass murder. Through the explanation of mental illness, White people who commit murder can assume or regain some standard of decency and be made less responsible via what Headley (2004) calls the "normativity of Whiteness" and resembles colour-blind logics (Bonilla-Silva, 2006/2014). To our knowledge, colour-blindness bounded within mental illness discourses is distinct in our data and provides new areas for understanding colour-blind logics.

Together, this mixed-methods research allows for both broad and nuanced analyses of understanding conversations about racism. As social environments with major influence in contemporary society, social media platforms are important spaces to analyse social psychological theories and processes like that of colour-blindness. Computational social science research allows researchers to process big data produced in these platforms and to conduct quicker and more reliable broad thematic analyses of the data. As this article shows, combining this broad analysis with a more nuanced discourse analysis of racially suppressive discourses allows an understanding for both broader themes and detailed understandings of how colour-blindness is present in German-speaking discussions about racism.

sections.paper_id: Windel et al. - 2023 - Color‐blind and racially suppressive discourses on German‐spe
paragraphs: This research has several limitations that constitute directions for further research. First, our data did not capture the discourses of structural, institutional racism/ethnocentrism that Black people, people of colour, and migrants living in Germany experience on a daily basis (Arndt & Ofuatey-Alazard, 2011;Sow, 2018). A second limitation is the focus on only German-language and mixed German-language tweets. The Hanau shootings impacted many communities in Germany, including the Kurdish and Roma community; we likely missed important tweets because of our selection for German-language tweets only.

These limitations suggest that further research is important. Future research should cast a wider net examining multiple languages on Twitter. It should also ask questions related to how those racialized as others in Germanspeaking countries discuss structural racism and lived experience of institutional racism, and as a follow-up on this study, how colour-blindness and racially-suppressive discourses in German-speaking countries are discussed and made sense of among Black people, people of colour, and migrants living in Germany.

sections.paper_id: Windel et al. - 2023 - Color‐blind and racially suppressive discourses on German‐spe
paragraphs: Despite these limitations, our mixed-method approach uncovered multifaceted public conversations in the aftermath of a White nationalist shooting and offers interpretations of large-scale conversations among German-speaking Twitter users. Furthermore, this study provides insights into discourses on racism within the culturally colour-blind German context. We are reluctant to conclude that there is a "new" way that people within a German-speaking context are making sense of and collectively understanding racism beyond invisibility, colour-blindness, and/or the work of extreme groups. Our data suggest that the naming of racism is multiform, and the nuances are subtle. Nevertheless, we think that Lentin's (2016) evocative description of the duality of racism is a useful analytical lens for situating the nature of race and racism in contemporary societies like that of Germany. Lentin argues that racism is both frozen and motile. Specifically, racism is frozen when viewed as a historical event like Nazi Germany as an isolated racist case and is motile and polyvalent-applied across contexts, constantly shifting, and a subject of debate and questioning. We think this potential interplay between frozen and motile racism-simultaneously nowhere and everywhere-may be generating new discourses on racism that includes seeing, naming, and legitimizing racism as an explanation (Degele, 2020). Indeed, Angela Merkel's acknowledgment that the Hanau shootings were a racist event and the proliferation of Black Lives Matter marches across German cities suggest a potential shift in the public discourse on racism (Degele, 2020). Yet, it remains unclear whether the German government will shift its structural investment and go "beyond lip-service in responding to racism" (Degele, 2020, p. 2). Despite these limitations, our mixed-method approach uncovered multifaceted public conversations in the aftermath of a White nationalist shooting and offers interpretations of large-scale conversations among German-speaking Twitter users. Furthermore, this study provides insights into discourses on racism within the culturally colour-blind German context. We are reluctant to conclude that there is a "new" way that people within a German-speaking context are making sense of and collectively understanding racism beyond invisibility, colour-blindness, and/or the work of extreme groups. Our data suggest that the naming of racism is multiform, and the nuances are subtle. Nevertheless, we think that Lentin's (2016) evocative description of the duality of racism is a useful analytical lens for situating the nature of race and racism in contemporary societies like that of Germany. Lentin argues that racism is both frozen and motile. Specifically, racism is frozen when viewed as a historical event like Nazi Germany as an isolated racist case and is motile and polyvalent-applied across contexts, constantly shifting, and a subject of debate and questioning. We think this potential interplay between frozen and motile racism-simultaneously nowhere and everywhere-may be generating new discourses on racism that includes seeing, naming, and legitimizing racism as an explanation (Degele, 2020). Indeed, Angela Merkel's acknowledgment that the Hanau shootings were a racist event and the proliferation of Black Lives Matter marches across German cities suggest a potential shift in the public discourse on racism (Degele, 2020). Yet, it remains unclear whether the German government will shift its structural investment and go "beyond lip-service in responding to racism" (Degele, 2020, p. 2).
